flashcards.front: What is a proposition in propositional logic?
flashcards.back: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either completely true or completely false, but not both. Examples: '2+2=4' (true), 'Delhi is the capital of India' (true), '5>7' (false).
A truth table is a mathematical table that shows all possible truth values of a compound proposition for every combination of truth values of its component propositions.

flashcards.front: Define logical negation (¬¨) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Negation (NOT) reverses the truth value. If p is true, ¬¨p is false; if p is false, ¬¨p is true. Truth table: T‚ÜíF, F‚ÜíT.

flashcards.front: Define logical conjunction (‚àß) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Conjunction (AND) is true only when both operands are true. Truth table: T‚àßT=T, T‚àßF=F, F‚àßT=F, F‚àßF=F.

flashcards.front: Define logical disjunction (‚à®) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Disjunction (OR) is false only when both operands are false. Truth table: T‚à®T=T, T‚à®F=T, F‚à®T=T, F‚à®F=F.

flashcards.front: Define implication (p‚Üíq) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Implication (p‚Üíq) is false only when p is true and q is false. Truth table: T‚ÜíT=T, T‚ÜíF=F, F‚ÜíT=T, F‚ÜíF=T. Also called 'if p then q'.

flashcards.front: Define biconditional (p‚Üîq) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Biconditional (p‚Üîq) is true when both operands have the same truth value. Truth table: T‚ÜîT=T, T‚ÜîF=F, F‚ÜîT=F, F‚ÜîF=T. Also called 'p if and only if q'.
A tautology is a compound proposition that is always true regardless of the truth values of its component propositions. Example: p‚à®¬¨p (Law of Excluded Middle).
A contradiction is a compound proposition that is always false regardless of the truth values of its component propositions. Example: p‚àß¬¨p.
A contingency is a compound proposition that is neither always true nor always false; its truth value depends on the truth values of its components.
Two propositions p and q are logically equivalent (p‚â°q) if they have identical truth values for all possible combinations of truth values of their components.

flashcards.front: State the Commutative Laws for ‚àß and ‚à®
flashcards.back: p‚àßq ‚â° q‚àßp and p‚à®q ‚â° q‚à®p. These laws state that the order of operands does not affect the result for AND and OR operations.

flashcards.front: State the Associative Laws for ‚àß and ‚à®
flashcards.back: (p‚àßq)‚àßr ‚â° p‚àß(q‚àßr) and (p‚à®q)‚à®r ‚â° p‚à®(q‚à®r). These laws state that grouping of operands does not affect the result for AND and OR operations.
¬¨(p‚àßq) ‚â° ¬¨p‚à®¬¨q and ¬¨(p‚à®q) ‚â° ¬¨p‚àß¬¨q. Negation of a conjunction is the disjunction of negations, and vice versa.
A compound proposition (or molecular statement) is formed by combining two or more propositions using logical connectives like ‚àß, ‚à®, ‚Üí, ‚Üî, and ¬¨.
